oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Status of Lories and Lorikeets in the US
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Touchton, Chris
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: This item originally appeared in AFA Watchbird Volume 50 Number 2 (April 2023).The following is an excerpt from the full PDF link.Status of Lories and Lorikeets in the United States By Chris TouchtonOver the years, lories have gotten harder and harder to find. Approximately five to six years ago, I told close Lory breeder friends that at the rate of decline, the main types of lories that would still be readily available in less than ten years, would be Blue Mountains, also known as Swainson’s, and , to a lesser degree, green-naped. Swainson’s are hardy birds and are one of the easiest to breed and green-naped, just because of the shear volume imported into the USA prior to the 1992 WBCA (Wild Bird Conservation Act) shutting down importation.I was right about the Swainson’s but wrong about the green-naped. It seems that the lory that is easiest to acquire at this time, is the Swainson’s and a fast reproducing Swainson’s x Forsten’s hybrid being sold as a pure bird.While the older breeders are either dying or opting to sell out, the number of new/younger breeders coming in to work with Lories does not match the number of exiting older breeders.Editor’s Note: Lories and lorikeets are small to medium sized parrots. Swainson’s or Blue Mountain (Trichoglossus haematodus moluccanus) lorikeets and green-naped (Trichoglossus haematodus) lories are just two of more than twenty types that are referred to as “rainbow,” with the green-naped being considered the most common. Green-naped lory babies with full cropsClick the PDF link associated with this item to view the complete item as originally published.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: AFA Watchbird; Vol. 50 No. 2 (2023): Watchbird April 2023; 16-18
